era_context: Thutmose III's imperial consolidation in the Levant was well underway, with Egyptian hegemony firmly established. Hatshepsut's power was at or near its peak, though the exact nature of her authority remains debated. The Mycenaean civilization continued to flourish in the Aegean. The Shang dynasty was in the middle of its reign in China. In South Asia, the Vedic period was underway.

events.title: Thutmose III's Military Consolidation in the Levant
events.description: Thutmose III continued his systematic campaigns to consolidate Egyptian control over the Levant. Multiple rebellions were suppressed, and the tribute system was further entrenched. The campaigns were characterized by rapid military movements and the use of advanced military technology, including chariots and coordinated infantry tactics. These campaigns established Egypt as the dominant military power in the eastern Mediterranean.

sources.name: Thutmose III's Annals at Karnak Temple

events.certainty_note: Thutmose III's annals record multiple campaigns, but the precise chronology and dating of individual campaigns is debated. The campaigns are generally dated to the early years of his reign, but exact year-by-year assignments are uncertain by ±2–3 years.
cross_references: Part of broader Thutmosid imperial expansion

events.title: Hatshepsut's Building Program and Temple Construction
events.description: Hatshepsut initiated or continued major building projects, including the construction of her mortuary temple at Deir el-Bahari. This temple is one of the most impressive monuments of ancient Egypt, featuring terraced colonnades and elaborate reliefs. The construction of such monuments required significant resources and demonstrated Hatshepsut's access to wealth and power. The temple also served as a vehicle for Hatshepsut's self-promotion and the legitimation of her rule.

sources.name: The Temple of Deir el-Bahari (archaeological evidence and inscriptions)

events.certainty_note: The construction of Hatshepsut's mortuary temple is well-attested through archaeological evidence and inscriptions. The temple was built over a period of years, and the exact dates of construction phases are debated, but the temple was largely completed by the end of Hatshepsut's reign (c. 1458 BCE).
cross_references: Part of Hatshepsut's consolidation of power and self-promotion

events.title: Mycenaean Trade Networks and Economic Expansion
events.description: The Mycenaean civilization was engaged in extensive trade networks throughout the Mediterranean and into the Near East. Mycenaean pottery and goods have been found in Egypt, the Levant, and throughout the Aegean. This trade was facilitated by the palace-centered economy and the use of ships for maritime commerce. The trade networks brought wealth and exotic goods to the Mycenaean world and facilitated cultural exchange.
key_figures: Mycenaean rulers and merchants (names not preserved in contemporary records)

sources.name: Linear B tablets recording trade and economic activities
Archaeological evidence of Mycenaean pottery and goods in Egypt and the Levant

events.certainty_note: The existence of Mycenaean trade networks is well-attested through archaeological evidence and Linear B records. The extent and nature of the trade is debated, but the presence of Mycenaean goods in Egypt and the Levant is confirmed.
cross_references: Contemporary with Thutmose III's reign in Egypt and Hatshepsut's building program

disconfirming_evidence: The extent of Egyptian control over the Levant may have been less comprehensive than Egyptian inscriptions suggest. Some scholars argue that local rulers maintained more autonomy than official records indicate, and that Egyptian control was limited to coastal areas and major cities. Additionally, the dating of Hatshepsut's building projects is debated, with some scholars placing the major construction of her mortuary temple later in her reign (c. 1470–1460 BCE) rather than in the late 1470s BCE.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1476 BCE is dominated by Egyptian sources, particularly the annals of Thutmose III and inscriptions related to Hatshepsut. The Mycenaean world is known primarily through archaeological evidence and Linear B tablets. The perspectives of Levantine populations and other contemporary civilizations are largely absent from the surviving record. The Egyptian record is shaped by royal propaganda and may overstate the extent of Egyptian control and military success.

graph_edges.note: Continued campaigns to consolidate Egyptian control in the Levant
Hatshepsut's building program continued and expanded throughout her reign
